transcript.text: Hello, hello.
Hey, Lisa, how's it going?
I'm good.
Apologies for being late.
I was driving back up from NVIDIA headquarters and it took a little longer.
Who would have thought traffic on a two o'clock on a Wednesday?
Yeah, that is a little bit surprising, but pretty cool location to be driving back from.
Yeah, absolutely.
Where are you based, Tyler?
Yeah, I'm based in Houston Tech.
And lived in SF for three years, and now I get to travel back there a lot more than I thought I would when I joined, so I love it.
Fantastic.
Well, I'm going to be in Houston, actually, in a couple of weeks myself, going out to take my husband for a surprise football game between Houston and Denver.
Oh, exciting.
Yeah.
His best friend lives out there, so it's like a birthday gift that we're going to do.
But I spent a little bit of time in Houston.
Are you originally from Houston?
Yeah, my fiancé and I are both from the Houston area, and that's what has us planning roots, you know, in this place that gets so hot in the summer that you wish you were anywhere else.
But it sounds like you've been to Houston a few times.
I have.
I will say, it's a sleeper city.
Like, it's not a typical, you go in and you're like, hit in the face of, here's the culture, here's the food scene, here's everything else.
Oh, sad.
Because my husband's best friend's there, we visited a couple of times.
And I'm like, oh, you've got some secret spots around here.
It's more like a bit of a treasure hunt in Houston.
So it's got its own cool vibe going on.
Oh, yeah, you know it.
The culture in Houston is hard to beat.
I mean, we've got Beyonce, right?
We've got Houston rap, which is like a genre of its own.
And then there's a lot of different cultures in Houston.
So the food, naturally, is wonderful.
What team will y'all be rooting for?
Will y'all be rooting for the Texans or the Broncos?
No, Broncos.
My husband's a big Broncos fan.
Well, no worries.
My dad is from Dallas.
And so I was cursed with the Cowboys as my team.
But it's great to meet you.
I know that we've only got 19 minutes left.
Do you have a hard stop?
do you have a little bit of extra time?
a little bit of extra time if we need it, if that works for you.
Yeah.
Okay, perfect.
Well, I'm the...
I'm founding account executive here at GrowthX, and I know that Marcel wanted to be on this call really badly, but he's got a super important demo with investors that we're talking to for Series B.
So it came up out of the blue, and luckily, I've been taking a lot of calls on his behalf, so you're in good hands, and I'm sure we'll loop Marcel back in for the follow-up.
Tell me a little bit more, Lisa, just about like, you know, your interest in learning more about GrowthX.
I know that Jai was the one who connected us, but I guess we'd love to, you know, just kind of get some initial context from you on where we might be able to help.
I think it is, Jai asked to meet and learn a little bit more to see if there's anything value that could help us in our next stage of growth.
So really, this is more of a curiosity, getting to know what is difficult to often glean off of a website.
Like, really, what is it focusing on?
What is it that you all are looking to help drive?
And where are you guys seeing success?
Things like that.
Yeah, let me give you that initial context.
So think about GrowthX as an agency, right, for simplicity, but we're end-to-end.
So SEO and GEO strategy, content creation, and executing on a 12-month roadmap.

A lot of what we do is editorial as a foundation, but the main focus is helping you drive higher-intent organic traffic and building right editorial content at scale by combining very sophisticated AI workflows with experts in the loop from content marketing, writing, and SEO.
background.
Does that help provide, like, that initial context?
I know the website has a lot of use cases and information.
Yes, it does.
So I guess tell me more about, yeah, your focus on the GEO side and who's helping you execute on that strategy.
So for us, I've got, I do have a vendor that I have brought on that is doing a lot of our content and organic and our LLM strategy.
So we are, we are working with someone.
I think the thing is, I'm interested to understand what the AI workflows that you have that are augmenting what would typically be done.
And then how well does that actually work, especially when it comes to the more technical content and nuances of it.

And that's one thing that I've just always seen with any vendor that we've ever worked with in the past from content, content strategy, content execution is, especially being an AI company, the tech, even for inside of DataRobot, like people who understand.
And the technology have a difficult time really writing about it with authority.
So I'm just I was just kind of curious to see, like, how you all are setting up to work in that kind of scenario.
Yeah, it makes a ton of sense.

And I think that's why some more technical companies in the B2B space have chosen us as a partner is because of how technical, right, our content creation can be, how specialized we can make, you know, what we generate for you.
And even when it is something that requires more expertise, we can really, really be a solution, right?
So what can create quite a bottleneck internally?
So just as an example, right, we work today with abnormal security, Surge AI, Augment Code.
So these are a few companies we work with that have more of a technical lens.
And in terms of our process, do you know much about, I guess, what more?
Yeah.
So did prior to starting GrowthX, because that would be a good foundation to kind of like segue into the process of how we create the content.
Yeah, no, that'd be great.
Perfect.
So Marcel, his story, he's always believed that brands should act more like publishers.
When he was at IBM, he recognized that, you know, at that time, it was like early 2010s, their paid advertising budget.
And his words, like, it was large enough for us to go out and buy a couple publications.

And so, you know, what he's wanted to do is build an organic content engine that builds trust with the audience so that you can then build distribution around that audience and then think about how to convert them into revenue.
And with paid, it's just like, so often you're renting the audience's attention, but now we're at a point where the LLMs is actually where you want to show up because clicks are disappearing.
The folks who come from AI search visits are showing much higher intent, they're spending a lot longer.
And so what he did in his career was every stop, he created some version of this content engine.
Like at IBM, he built this website called securityintelligence.com.
It was live for over 10 years, and they just folded it back into the parent website.
That, you know, drove $17 million in revenue in the first year and won Best B2B Website Award.
And so at the next place, HP, he built a newsletter called AI Minds.
He had over 1,000 experts contribute.
At HashiCorp, he was there from $6 million to $100 million.
At Scale, he was the CMO leading into the acquisition by Facebook.
And so for every stop in his career, he had gone through this process.
And at DeepGram, he built AI workflows to see if it was possible to do that without building this massive team.
And so the success at DeepGram, he put...
But on these workshops, and there were so many people that were like, could you just do this for me?
That he created GrowthX in the process, and that's a little bit of the origin story to show you, like, the expertise, right, around what we're working on.
Okay.
Talk to you a bit about the AI aspect of this, yeah.
Yeah, 100%.
So our process, what we do is in phase one, we're focused on generating artifacts that can give the context engineering necessary for the workflows to be effective.
And then in phase two, we're getting to daily publishing.

So in that phase one process, what we'll do is we'll do our own deep research and SEO audit, we'll set up a kickoff call and ingest different resources that you have, brand documents, sales, decks, or transcripts.
And then we'll start to develop these artifacts around.
Your company, the ICP that you care about, so different audience personas, artifacts, and then writing guidelines and, you know, the brand voice so that it can be created right in your tone.

And then when you look at, like, an artifact or when you look at a workflow in particular where we're creating content, Biologica is a good example because they're a customer that is in the supplement space.
And so they would have, like, their chief medical officer review every single piece of content that we created for accuracy.

And at a certain point, you know, we built that expert review in as a step in our AI workflows because we had enough context, we had enough recordings and documentation to basically understand what the gotchas are.
And so, you know, just looking at this example here, each step, we've built this workflow in code so that anytime a model changes, anytime we need to make an adjustment, we can move super fast.
And we
We can even build, you know, custom workflows like you're seeing with the expert review here.
But let's take this brief generation.
We're starting with a keyword, a direction, and the artifacts that I mentioned.
And then in this step, we're actually doing a lot more than what you're seeing in this view right here.
We are going in and grabbing keyword traffic.
We're looking at SERP ranking factors and competitor content.
And then all of that informs really robust brief so that we can then create content off of this assignment.
And so, you know, step one is the brief, but then the research step, you'll see that we generate research questions.
And for every question, we do deep research and validate against all of these third-party sources.
And so there's a lot to, you know, how we actually build comprehensive content, how when we...
We an article draft.
We're able to go in and do deep research on every single passage in that article to ensure that it's accurate against a certain confidence interval.
And if it's not, then we'll actually suggest a rewrite.

But then you've also got our experts who are able to make changes to the content as it's being drafted, leverage the AI and the interface to, you know, make, to provide instructions against the artifacts that we have that provide the right context.
And so, you know, it's much more than like going into ChatGPT and I'm asking it to generate an article.
This is connecting to several different providers' APIs.
APIs, you know, we're leveraging hundreds of thousands, if not millions of tokens, and we're generating, you know, over 10 million words a month for all of our customers.
Go to, talk to me a little bit about.
The confidence intervals and how those work and how you set those up and like how you can tell it's actually doing well.
Yeah, to be honest with you, I feel like that would be a good question to ask Marcel in the follow-up.

I don't know that deep into the technology, but I can show you a lot more around examples of content that we've created or, you know, different workflows outside of this one in particular, whatever would be most helpful for you.

I'm curious to, so one, I'm curious on the authority of the ranking, like making sure, like I know you've been doing deep research and you're doing citations and things like that, but I'm curious to then understand how do you work in a space like ours where content doesn't exist, there isn't something that talks about it.
So for example, an agent AI lifecycle, like that, of course we can do some deep research.
the comes I'm curious, really
You can use ChatGPT, but you can use an LLM to create it, but there isn't an agent lifecycle yet.
There is no standardization for it, but we're setting the tone for creating what that is.
There are certain concepts that just don't generally exist quite yet, but we are developing them on, because we're an AI company, so you're at the tip of the spear of what it is.
So how does your content generation then deal with topics that just literally don't exist right now?
Like, you can't do deep research on stuff that doesn't exist quite yet.
Like, this is where, I'm very curious, this is where the technical stuff starts to get a little bit tricky for us.
I mean, everyone is like, oh, they've asked me multiple times, even internally at DataRobot, like, well, can't you use ChatGPT to create content faster?
I'm like, I absolutely can, but it's never heard of the content we're trying to create.
Like Pareto-optimized workflows of LLMs using your data to benchmark against cost, accuracy, and latency is not a concept that exists.
It's something we're doing.
So, I mean, I can ask ChatGPT.
It's going to make up an answer for me, and LLM is going to make up an answer for me, but it's just not a technology that exists.
So how do I ask it to write stuff for me?
And so I'm curious to see if, like, maybe we're the one or the only companies that are in that kind of a world or other companies that you've worked with where they're creating a net new market.
So it's like, how do you do, how does your technology then support that?
Yeah, I mean, very valid question.
I think the best way to answer it, you know, would be to, like, go into how we decide what we're going to write about, right?
So once we have the persona artifact created...
This is basically to get in the mind of your ICP, right?
Because we're trying to tackle this from the perspective of, let's just go back to the beginning here.
You know, how would that audience, how would that ICP go in and connect with ChatGBT if they were looking for software built for them?
You know, it's not software for XYZ.
It's like, here's my whole story.
And so we've got this artifact that says, what is this person's fears and uncertainties?
What are their motivations?
How do you win or lose their trust?
What's going to get them promoted or fired?
And then we're also going to connect that to the topics that you want to be the expert in.
And so for Engine, they're in the business travel space, know, travel booking and reservations, travel management, travel trends, all of the assignments that we're thinking about.
it.
like
Content that we're creating, they're in the lens of these topics, and they're in the lens of the audience that you're targeting.
And then we're looking at search volume, keyword difficulty, where are your competitors, right, doing really well, or where are their opportunities to get ahead.

And then the combination of those things allows us to think about content that would be relevant to your audience, so that you can start showing up in the answers that they are looking for, you know, insights around.

And, you know, just to show you like a tactical example here, for Augment Code, we've built out this entire guide section of their site, and it has 3x the traffic of the blog that they are working on with their own team.
And a lot of the content in the guide section, it's, you know, they're in the AI coding space, so it's everything connected.
To AI coding, that could be thought leadership, that could be this tool versus that tool.

But what you see with Augment Code, and we've been able to drive their traffic really quickly in a short amount of time, is that for the target keywords, you know, they're starting to see our answers show up in the citations.

So they've got three different citations from these guides that we've built, and they're also starting to show up in the answer for what they want to be connected to, you know, more complex enterprise code bases, context understanding, AI coding tools like Augment Code, Augment Code.

And so, you know, we're not going to be able to create content about topics that aren't around yet, but what we can do is create content around the topics that you want to be the expert in, that your audience cares about.
And thanks for bearing with me here.

And, you know, our workflows are using a variety of providers.
So it's like Perplexity's Deep Research or ChatGPT.
We use Exa and Tably in part of the workflows as well.
And we're also thinking in terms of retrieval so that it can be really easy to grab the right information at the right time.
That was a novel, but I feel like, you know, it's a long answer to the question that you posed.
No, could you send me a link to the guides?

It's much more technical.

That's along the lines of, I'm assuming that's the level of technicality of content that we would be working with, which has always been the struggle for us as an organization where we have to manually write a lot of that.
So that would be helpful.

It's always the SME's reviews of everything that become the bottleneck.
Yes.

I think I saw you have some images or things like that.

Does your tooling provide help with that?

So that's the other thing.
Then you're starting to compound the.
Problem, both of the design and the throughput perspective.
Yeah.

We'll have the articles drafted, but we would lean on your team to be able to approve the drafts and make sure that we're ready to get it on your site.
Yeah.
Okay.

we...
